context: TypeScript codebase. ReservationManager is a known shallow module the team wants grilled directly. It has methods: createReservation(customerId, restaurantId, partySize, date, time, smoking, vip, depositCents, depositRequired, notes) and updateReservation(id, partialFields). Three callers across api/reservations.ts, jobs/reminders.ts, and admin/reservations.ts each duplicate input validation (party size > 0, date in the future, deposit >= 0). The interface leaks Stripe payment intent IDs into the create signature even though only one caller uses deposits.
expected_behavior: Skips Phase 1 (--no-explore is set) and goes directly to Phase 3 grilling on ReservationManager
Phase 3a frames the candidate concretely — names the existing interface, problem (10-arg create + 3 boolean state), and the user-visible target shape — before proposing alternatives

Phase 3b designs the interface TWICE: produces at least two distinct alternatives (e.g., 'Minimal' vs 'Caller-optimised' vs 'Ports-and-Adapters'), each with its own dependency strategy and trade-offs. Naming alternatives A/B/C/D is encouraged but the load-bearing rule is plurality, not the labels
Classifies dependency category for each alternative (in-process / local-substitutable / remote-owned / true-external) — Stripe is true-external, restaurant DB is local-substitutable
Proposes promoting the scattered party-size / date / deposit invariants into the module (locality win) and hiding Stripe payment intent IDs behind a Deposit type (leverage win)
Phase 3c makes an OPINIONATED recommendation among the alternatives. Recommending without picking a winner is failure. The recommendation cites trade-offs explicitly
Names a seam (the deposit-collection step) and explicitly notes whether one adapter or two are expected — invokes the 'two adapters means a real seam' rule

Phase 3 hand-off output (3e) includes: implementation plan suitable for /bruhs:cook, test plan tied to the new interface (what becomes integration-testable / unit-testable / contract-testable), migration plan from the old interface, and a tests-to-delete list
If a rejection reason is durable, drafts an ADR — does not auto-skip ADR creation when it would prevent future re-suggestion
Still does not refactor code or write the new module — output is the co-designed interface + dependency strategy + plans
failure_modes: Designs ONE interface only — violates the 'design-it-twice' rule (Phase 3b)
Presents alternatives but refuses to pick a winner — 'recommending without an opinion is failure' (Phase 3c)
Adds a port (interface + adapter) for a single implementation, violating 'two adapters means a real seam'
Writes the actual TypeScript implementation of ReservationManager v2
Skips the hand-off bundle (Phase 3e) — produces an interface design with no implementation plan, test plan, or tests-to-delete list
Refuses to draft an ADR even when a load-bearing rejection reason would justify one
